inputs.story: A new reality television series aims to follow the pursuits of several gold miners. Using only the clues that follow, match each miner to his claim and location, and determine how many ounces of gold he uncovered this year (in ounces). Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
inputs.constraints:
1. The prospector who found 11 ounces of gold was either the prospector working at Fuller Rise or the prospector working at Culver Gorge.
2. The prospector who found 20 ounces of gold worked at the Fuller Rise claim.
3. Ivan Ingram found 14 ounces of gold.
4. The miner working at Belle Hart finished with 6 ounces more gold than Fred Fletcher.
5. Jack Jacobs finished with somewhat less gold than Gil Gonzalez.
inputs.constants:
miners: Fred Fletcher; Gil Gonzalez; Ivan Ingram; Jack Jacobs.
ounces: 11; 14; 17; 20.
claims: Belle Hart; Culver Gorge; Fuller Rise; York River.
inputs.solution:
miners	ounces	claims
Jack Jacobs	17	Belle Hart
Fred Fletcher	11	Culver Gorge
Gil Gonzalez	20	Fuller Rise 
Ivan Ingram	14	York River

asp_generation.prompt:
<task>
You are given a logic grid puzzle with a story, numbered clues, and categorized constants. Your task is to generate a complete ASP (Answer Set Programming) program that, when solved with Clingo, yields a unique answer set representing the puzzle's solution.

Provided below are:
- two examples: `example_1` and `example_2`,
- the problem you must solve: `problem_to_solve`,
- important rules: `rules`.
</task>

<example_1>
"Against the Grain" offers hand-made wooden furniture at reasonable prices. Each item is made by an in-house employee. Using only the clues that follow, match each item to the employee who crafted it, and determine its price and the type of wood used to make it. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Constants:
Employees: Bonita; Yvette; Tabitha.
Prices: $225; $275; $325.
Wood types: ash; poplar; sandalwood.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
employee("Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha").
price(225; 275; 325).
wood_type("ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood").

% Search space: for each employee E, assign exactly one price P and wood type W.
{match(E, P, W): price(P), wood_type(W)}=1 :- employee(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; P1=P2; W1=W2}=0 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), (E1,P1,W1)!=(E2,P2,W2).

% 1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
P=325 :- match(E,P,W), E="Bonita".

% 2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".

% 3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".

% 4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.
{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.

#show match/3.
```
</example_1>

<example_2>
The Winter Olympics have just wrapped up in Norway. Using only the clues that follow, determine the number of gold and silver medals won by each country. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The four teams are the team from Bolivia, the team that won 3 gold medals, the team that won 6 silver medals, and the team from Argentina.
2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams.
5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.

Constants:
Countries: Argentina; Bolivia; Grenada; Oman.
Silver medals: 2; 6; 10; 11.
Gold medals: 1; 2; 3; 4.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
country("Argentina"; "Bolivia"; "Grenada"; "Oman").
silver_medals(2; 6; 10; 11).
gold_medals(1; 2; 3; 4).

% Search space: for each country C, assign exactly one silver medal count S and gold medal count G.
{assign(C, S, G): silver_medals(S), gold_medals(G)}=1 :- country(C).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{C1=C2; S1=S2; G1=G2}=0 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), (C1,S1,G1)!=(C2,S2,G2).

% 1. The four teams are all different — Bolivia, the 3-gold team, the 6-silver team, and Argentina each refer to distinct countries.
{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).

% 2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Oman", S2=10.

% 3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
{G=2; G=1}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), C="Oman".

% 4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams — three pairwise inequalities.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C2!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C1!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".

% 5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
{C="Argentina"; S=2}=0 :- assign(C,S,G), G=4.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Argentina", S2=2.

% 6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.
{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.

#show assign/3.
```
</example_2>

Here is the problem to be solved:
<problem_to_solve>
A new reality television series aims to follow the pursuits of several gold miners. Using only the clues that follow, match each miner to his claim and location, and determine how many ounces of gold he uncovered this year (in ounces). Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The prospector who found 11 ounces of gold was either the prospector working at Fuller Rise or the prospector working at Culver Gorge.
2. The prospector who found 20 ounces of gold worked at the Fuller Rise claim.
3. Ivan Ingram found 14 ounces of gold.
4. The miner working at Belle Hart finished with 6 ounces more gold than Fred Fletcher.
5. Jack Jacobs finished with somewhat less gold than Gil Gonzalez.

Constants:
miners: Fred Fletcher; Gil Gonzalez; Ivan Ingram; Jack Jacobs.
ounces: 11; 14; 17; 20.
claims: Belle Hart; Culver Gorge; Fuller Rise; York River.
</problem_to_solve>

Here are some important rules to follow when generating the ASP code:
<rules>
1. Constant declarations — "category(const1; const2; ...)."
   - Category names: lowercase with underscores only (e.g., wood_type, gold_medals).
   - All constants in the same category must use the same format — all integers or all quoted strings.
   - Prefer integers: strip special characters ($225 → 225), strip units while keeping the number ("3 days" → 3, "2 months" → 2), and convert verbal or ordinal numerals (once → 1, twice → 2, third → 3, September → 9).
   - Use double-quoted strings when no integer representation is possible (e.g., "Bonita", "9:30AM").

2. Predicates
   - Use the minimum number of predicates needed to relate all categories.
   - Every category must appear in at least one predicate argument; all arguments must be distinct variables.
   - Choose a name that reflects the relationship (e.g., match, assign, schedule, launch).

3. Search space — one choice rule per predicate, anchoring on one category in the body and ranging over the rest inside the braces:
     {pred(V1, V2, ...): cat2(V2), cat3(V3), ...}=1 :- cat1(V1).

4. Uniqueness — no value in any non-anchor category appears more than once:
     {V1=V2; W1=W2; ...}=0 :- pred(V1,W1,...), pred(V2,W2,...), (V1,W1,...)!=(V2,W2,...).

5. Constraint rules — encode each clue using one or more rules of these forms, using only defined constants and predicates:
     <C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.      % if all Li hold, at least one Ci must hold
     {<C1>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.  % if all Li hold, exactly k of Ci hold
     :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.                        % this combination is forbidden

   Common patterns:
   - "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D" → three rules: A≠B, A is C or D, B is C or D.
   - "A, B and C are all different" → three pairwise inequality rules (A≠B, A≠C, B≠C).
   - "Neither A nor B is X" → {A_cond; B_cond}=0 :- ... X ... .

6. Show directives — one "#show pred/arity." per predicate.
</rules>

ASP Program:
asp_generation.thinking:
Here's a thinking process:

1.  **Understand the Goal**: The user wants an ASP (Answer Set Programming) program that solves a given logic grid puzzle. The program should be written in a specific format, following provided rules and examples.

2.  **Analyze the Problem**:
   - **Story**: Reality TV show about gold miners. Match each miner to his claim, location, and ounces of gold found.
   - **Categories**:
     - Miners: Fred Fletcher, Gil Gonzalez, Ivan Ingram, Jack Jacobs
     - Ounces: 11, 14, 17, 20
     - Claims: Belle Hart, Culver Gorge, Fuller Rise, York River
   - **Clues**:
     1. The prospector who found 11 ounces was either the prospector working at Fuller Rise or the prospector working at Culver Gorge.
     2. The prospector who found 20 ounces worked at the Fuller Rise claim.
     3. Ivan Ingram found 14 ounces of gold.
     4. The miner working at Belle Hart finished with 6 ounces more gold than Fred Fletcher.
     5. Jack Jacobs finished with somewhat less gold than Gil Gonzalez.

3.  **Map to ASP Concepts**:
   - **Constants**:
     - `miner("Fred Fletcher"; "Gil Gonzalez"; "Ivan Ingram"; "Jack Jacobs").`
     - `ounces(11; 14; 17; 20).`
     - `claim("Belle Hart"; "Culver Gorge"; "Fuller Rise"; "York River").`
   - **Predicate**: `assign(M, O, C)` where M is miner, O is ounces, C is claim.
   - **Search Space**: `{assign(M, O, C): ounces(O), claim(C)} = 1 :- miner(M).`
   - **Uniqueness**: `{M1=M2; O1=O2; C1=C2}=0 :- assign(M1,O1,C1), assign(M2,O2,C2), (M1,O1,C1) != (M2,O2,C2).`
   - **Clue Encoding**:
     1. `O=11 :- assign(M,O,C), C="Fuller Rise".` or `C="Culver Gorge".` Wait, the clue says "either ... or ...". In logic puzzles, this usually means exactly one of them is true for that specific person, or it means the 11-ounce miner is at Fuller Rise OR Culver Gorge. Let's interpret it as: `O=11` implies `C="Fuller Rise" ; C="Culver Gorge"`. Or using the `{C1; ...}=k` form: `{C="Fuller Rise"; C="Culver Gorge"}=1 :- assign(M,O,C), O=11.`
     2. `O=20 :- assign(M,O,C), C="Fuller Rise".` (Actually, it's a direct mapping: `C="Fuller Rise" :- assign(M,O,C), O=20.` or `O=20 :- assign(M,O,C), C="Fuller Rise".` Both work. I'll use `O=20 :- assign(M,O,C), C="Fuller Rise".`)
     3. `O=14 :- assign(M,O,C), M="Ivan Ingram".`
     4. "The miner working at Belle Hart finished with 6 ounces more gold than Fred Fletcher."
        Let `O_bh` be ounces for Belle Hart, `O_ff` be ounces for Fred Fletcher.
        `O_bh = O_ff + 6 :- assign(M1,O1,C1), assign(M2,O2,C2), C1="Belle Hart", M2="Fred Fletcher".`
        Wait, this is a constraint/implication. Actually, it's better to express it as a relation: `O1 = O2 + 6 :- assign(M1,O1,C1), assign(M2,O2,C2), C1="Belle Hart", M2="Fred Fletcher".`
     5. "Jack Jacobs finished with somewhat less gold than Gil Gonzalez."
        `O_jj < O_gg :- assign(M1,O1,C1), assign(M2,O2,C2), M1="Jack Jacobs", M2="Gil Gonzalez".`

   Let's verify the clues against the puzzle type. Usually, "either A or B" in these puzzles means exactly one of the two conditions applies to the entity mentioned. So `{C="Fuller Rise"; C="Culver Gorge"}=1 :- assign(M,O,C), O=11.` is correct.
   Clue 2: "The prospector who found 20 ounces of gold worked at the Fuller Rise claim." -> `O=20 :- assign(M,O,C), C="Fuller Rise".`
   Clue 3: "Ivan Ingram found 14 ounces of gold." -> `O=14 :- assign(M,O,C), M="Ivan Ingram".`
   Clue 4: "The miner working at Belle Hart finished with 6 ounces more gold than Fred Fletcher." -> `O1 = O2 + 6 :- assign(M1,O1,C1), assign(M2,O2,C2), C1="Belle Hart", M2="Fred Fletcher".`
   Clue 5: "Jack Jacobs finished with somewhat less gold than Gil Gonzalez." -> `O1 < O2 :- assign(M1,O1,C1), assign(M2,O2,C2), M1="Jack Jacobs", M2="Gil Gonzalez".`

   Wait, let's check if the clues are fully consistent and lead to a unique solution.
   Miners: Fred, Gil, Ivan, Jack
   Ounces: 11, 14, 17, 20
   Claims: Belle Hart, Culver Gorge, Fuller Rise, York River

   From Clue 3: Ivan = 14.
   From Clue 2: Fuller Rise = 20.
   From Clue 1: 11 is either Fuller Rise or Culver Gorge. Since Fuller Rise is 20, 11 must be Culver Gorge. So Culver Gorge = 11.
   From Clue 4: Belle Hart = Fred + 6. Available ounces: 11, 14, 17, 20. Fred can't be 20 (Belle Hart would be 26, not available). Fred can't be 17 (Belle Hart 23). Fred can't be 14 (Ivan is 14). So Fred must be 11. Then Belle Hart = 17.
   So far:
   Ivan = 14
   Fuller Rise = 20
   Culver Gorge = 11
   Fred = 11
   Belle Hart = 17
   Remaining ounces: 20 (Fuller Rise), 17 (Belle Hart), 11 (Culver Gorge), 14 (Ivan).
   Remaining claims: Fuller Rise, Culver Gorge, Belle Hart, York River.
   Fred is at Culver Gorge (11).
   Ivan is at ? (14). Could be York River.
   Belle Hart claim has 17. Who is at Belle Hart? Not Ivan (14), not Fred (11). Must be Gil or Jack.
   Fuller Rise claim has 20.
   Let's check Clue 5: Jack < Gil.
   Ounces left for Gil and Jack: 17 and 20? Or 14 and 17? Wait, Ivan is 14. Fred is 11. So Gil and Jack must be 17 and 20.
   Since Jack < Gil, Jack = 17, Gil = 20.
   So:
   Jack = 17 -> Belle Hart claim.
   Gil = 20 -> Fuller Rise claim.
   Ivan = 14 -> York River claim.
   Fred = 11 -> Culver Gorge claim.
   All constraints satisfied. Unique solution.

   Now, encode into ASP properly following the rules.

   **Constants**:
   ```asp
   miner("Fred Fletcher"; "Gil Gonzalez"; "Ivan Ingram"; "Jack Jacobs").
   ounces(11; 14; 17; 20).
   claim("Belle Hart"; "Culver Gorge"; "Fuller Rise"; "York River").
   ```

   **Search Space**:
   ```asp
   {assign(M, O, C): ounces(O), claim(C)} = 1 :- miner(M).
   ```

   **Uniqueness**:
   ```asp
   {M1=M2; O1=O2; C1=C2}=0 :- assign(M1,O1,C1), assign(M2,O2,C2), (M1,O1,C1)!=(M2,O2,C2).
   ```

   **Clues**:
   1. `{C="Fuller Rise"; C="Culver Gorge"}=1 :- assign(M,O,C), O=11.`
   2. `O=20 :- assign(M,O,C), C="Fuller Rise".`
   3. `O=14 :- assign(M,O,C), M="Ivan Ingram".`
   4. `O1 = O2 + 6 :- assign(M1,O1,C1), assign(M2,O2,C2), C1="Belle Hart", M2="Fred Fletcher".`
   5. `O1 < O2 :- assign(M1,O1,C1), assign(M2,O2,C2), M1="Jack Jacobs", M2="Gil Gonzalez".`

   Wait, rule 5 says "somewhat less gold", which just means strictly less. `O1 < O2` is fine.
   Let's double check the exact wording for Clue 4: "The miner working at Belle Hart finished with 6 ounces more gold than Fred Fletcher."
   This implies: `O_belle = O_fred + 6`.
   My encoding: `O1 = O2 + 6 :- assign(M1,O1,C1), assign(M2,O2,C2), C1="Belle Hart", M2="Fred Fletcher".` This correctly links the specific miner at Belle Hart and Fred Fletcher.

   One minor point: In ASP, equality constraints like `O1 = O2 + 6` are valid in body/head. But sometimes it's safer to use `:- assign(M1,O1,C1), assign(M2,O2,C2), C1="Belle Hart", M2="Fred Fletcher", O1 != O2 + 6.` as a constraint, or keep it as a rule `O1 = O2 + 6 :- ...` which forces the head. Both work. I'll stick to `O1 = O2 + 6 :- ...` as it's standard.

   Let's verify the format matches the examples.
   Example 1 uses:
   `P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".`
   `P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".`
   So `O1 = O2 + 6 :- assign(M1,O1,C1), assign(M2,O2,C2), C1="Belle Hart", M2="Fred Fletcher".` is perfectly fine.

   Check Clue 1: "The prospector who found 11 ounces of gold was either the prospector working at Fuller Rise or the prospector working at Culver Gorge."
   Encoding: `{C="Fuller Rise"; C="Culver Gorge"}=1 :- assign(M,O,C), O=11.`
   This matches the pattern `{C1; ...}=k :- ...`.

   Check Clue 2: "The prospector who found 20 ounces of gold worked at the Fuller Rise claim."
   Encoding: `O=20 :- assign(M,O,C), C="Fuller Rise".`
   Or `C="Fuller Rise" :- assign(M,O,C), O=20.` Both are fine. I'll use `O=20 :- assign(M,O,C), C="Fuller Rise".`

   Check Clue 3: "Ivan Ingram found 14 ounces of gold."
   Encoding: `O=14 :- assign(M,O,C), M="Ivan Ingram".`

   Check Clue 5: "Jack Jacobs finished with somewhat less gold than Gil Gonzalez."
   Encoding: `O1 < O2 :- assign(M1,O1,C1), assign(M2,O2,C2), M1="Jack Jacobs", M2="Gil Gonzalez".`

   All looks solid.

   One detail: The uniqueness rule in the examples uses `{V1=V2; ...}=0 :- pred(...), pred(...), (V1,...)!=(V2,...).`
   I will follow that exactly.

   Let's assemble the full program.

   ```asp
   % Constants
   miner("Fred Fletcher"; "Gil Gonzalez"; "Ivan Ingram"; "Jack Jacobs").
   ounces(11; 14; 17; 20).
   claim("Belle Hart"; "Culver Gorge"; "Fuller Rise"; "York River").

   % Search space
   {assign(M, O, C): ounces(O), claim(C)} = 1 :- miner(M).

   % Uniqueness
   {M1=M2; O1=O2; C1=C2}=0 :- assign(M1,O1,C1), assign(M2,O2,C2), (M1,O1,C1)!=(M2,O2,C2).

   % 1. The prospector who found 11 ounces of gold was either the prospector working at Fuller Rise or the prospector working at Culver Gorge.
   {C="Fuller Rise"; C="Culver Gorge"}=1 :- assign(M,O,C), O=11.

   % 2. The prospector who found 20 ounces of gold worked at the Fuller Rise claim.
   O=20 :- assign(M,O,C), C="Fuller Rise".

   % 3. Ivan Ingram found 14 ounces of gold.
   O=14 :- assign(M,O,C), M="Ivan Ingram".

   % 4. The miner working at Belle Hart finished with 6 ounces more gold than Fred Fletcher.
   O1 = O2 + 6 :- assign(M1,O1,C1), assign(M2,O2,C2), C1="Belle Hart", M2="Fred Fletcher".

   % 5. Jack Jacobs finished with somewhat less gold than Gil Gonzalez.
   O1 < O2 :- assign(M1,O1,C1), assign(M2,O2,C2), M1="Jack Jacobs", M2="Gil Gonzalez".

   #show assign/3.
   ```

   Wait, let's verify if `O1 = O2 + 6` works correctly in Clingo when used as a head rule. Yes, it's a constraint on the head values. Alternatively, I could write it as `:- assign(M1,O1,C1), assign(M2,O2,C2), C1="Belle Hart", M2="Fred Fletcher", O1 != O2 + 6.` but the head rule is fine and matches example 1's `P1=P2-50`.

   One thing: In example 1, clue 4 is `{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.`
   My clue 1 matches this pattern.

   Let's double check clue 4's encoding. In logic grid puzzles, "A is B" usually means they are the same entity. Here, "The miner working at Belle Hart" and "Fred Fletcher" are two descriptions of potentially the same person, but the clue gives a numerical relationship between their ounces. It doesn't say they are the same person. It says: `ounces(BelleHartMiner) = ounces(FredFletcher) + 6`. This is correctly encoded.

   Check clue 5: "Jack Jacobs finished with somewhat less gold than Gil Gonzalez." -> `O1 < O2 :- assign(M1,O1,C1), assign(M2,O2,C2), M1="Jack Jacobs", M2="Gil Gonzalez".` Correct.

   One minor adjustment: In ASP, it's often cleaner to put the numeric comparison in the body as a constraint if it's meant to restrict solutions, but head rules are perfectly valid. I'll stick with head rules as they are used in the examples.

   Let's verify the uniqueness rule syntax from the prompt:
   `{V1=V2; W1=W2; ...}=0 :- pred(V1,W1,...), pred(V2,W2,...), (V1,W1,...)!=(V2,W2,...).`
   My code: `{M1=M2; O1=O2; C1=C2}=0 :- assign(M1,O1,C1), assign(M2,O2,C2), (M1,O1,C1)!=(M2,O2,C2).` Matches.

   Check constants formatting:
   Miners: strings with spaces. `"Fred Fletcher"` etc.
   Ounces: integers. `11; 14; 17; 20.`
   Claims: strings with spaces. `"Belle Hart"` etc.
   All good.

   "All constants in the same category must use the same format — all integers or all quoted strings." -> Ounces are integers, others are strings. Good.
   "Prefer integers: strip special characters..." -> Already done.

   Check clue 1 again: "The prospector who found 11 ounces of gold was either the prospector working at Fuller Rise or the prospector working at Culver Gorge."
   My encoding: `{C="Fuller Rise"; C="Culver Gorge"}=1 :- assign(M,O,C), O=11.`
   This says for the miner with 11 ounces, exactly one of those claims applies. Since each miner gets exactly one claim, this is effectively `C="Fuller Rise" ; C="Culver Gorge"`. The `{...}=1` form is correct per the rules.

   Check clue 2: "The prospector who found 20 ounces of gold worked at the Fuller Rise claim."
   My encoding: `O=20 :- assign(M,O,C), C="Fuller Rise".`
   This forces that if a miner is at Fuller Rise, they have 20 ounces. Since each claim is assigned exactly once, this works. Alternatively, `C="Fuller Rise" :- assign(M,O,C), O=20.` is also fine. I'll keep `O=20 :- ...`.

   Check clue 3: `O=14 :- assign(M,O,C), M="Ivan Ingram".` Good.

   Check clue 4: `O1 = O2 + 6 :- assign(M1,O1,C1), assign(M2,O2,C2), C1="Belle Hart", M2="Fred Fletcher".` Good.

   Check clue 5: `O1 < O2 :- assign(M1,O1,C1), assign(M2,O2,C2), M1="Jack Jacobs", M2="Gil Gonzalez".` Good.

   All clues are encoded. The program should yield a unique answer set.
   I will structure it exactly like the examples.

   One detail: In Clingo, `O1 = O2 + 6` might need to be `O1 == O2 + 6` or just `O1 = O2 + 6`. Both work. I'll stick to `O1 = O2 + 6`.
   Actually, in ASP, arithmetic in the head is fine.
   I'll format it carefully.

   Let's produce the final ASP code.
   I will add comments to match the style.
   Check if any clues need pairwise inequalities? No.
   Check if uniqueness rule covers all categories? Yes, M, O, C.
   Check show directive: `#show assign/3.`

   One minor point: In example 2, clue 1 uses `{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).`
   My clue 1 is similar.

   Everything looks solid. I will generate the response. 
   I'll double check the exact wording of the prompt's rules for uniqueness:
   `{V1=V2; W1=W2; ...}=0 :- pred(V1,W1,...), pred(V2,W2,...), (V1,W1,...)!=(V2,W2,...).`
   I'll follow exactly.

   Final check of the code:
   ```asp
   % Constants
   miner("Fred Fletcher"; "Gil Gonzalez"; "Ivan Ingram"; "Jack Jacobs").
   ounces(11; 14; 17; 20).
   claim("Belle Hart"; "Culver Gorge"; "Fuller Rise"; "York River").

   % Search space
   {assign(M, O, C): ounces(O), claim(C)} = 1 :- miner(M).

   % Uniqueness

#show assign/3.
   ```
